definitions.text: Any insect of the order Diptera; characterized by having two wings (except for some wingless species), also called true flies.
To travel through the air, another gas, or a vacuum, without being in contact with a grounded surface.
examples: Birds of passage fly to warmer regions as it gets colder in winter.
The Concorde flew from Paris to New York faster than any other passenger airplane.
Fly, my lord! The enemy are upon us!

definitions.text: To cause to fly (travel or float in the air): to transport via air or the like.
examples: Charles Lindbergh flew his airplane The Spirit of St. Louis across the Atlantic ocean.
Why don’t you go outside and fly kites, kids? The wind is just perfect.
There was a good wind, so I decided to give the kite a fly.

definitions.text: To hit a fly ball; to hit a fly ball that is caught for an out. Compare ground (verb) and line (verb).
examples: Jones flied to right in his last at-bat.

definitions.text: Well dressed, smart in appearance; in style, cool.

examples: The bullet barely grazed the wild fowl's fly.

definitions.text: Alternative form of vly (“swamp (in New York)”).
